issues.title: Need to figure out how to identify devices
issues.author: rgwilton
issues.body:
Need to be able to identify sources:
- Model currently defines as source-id, and defines it as a string, but perhaps a union or more complex type is needed.
- Benoit: perhaps this is not the right place to solve this.


Also, considering  when data is configured separate and pushed up from the devices.  But still need a generic way of identifying the data.

comments.author: bclaise
comments.body:
This is covered by the section 4 "Relationship to the Hardware YANG Data Model" in the GREEN YANG model.
But also by  section 3.1.2 "Controller vs. Device Initiated" in the GREEN framework

issues.title: For sources, is this a read/write property or a read-only property
issues.author: rgwilton
issues.body: I.e., do you have to configure all the sources within this model, or can they come from somewhere else.

comments.author: bclaise
comments.body:
In the GREEN framework, we should better explain read/write in section 3.1.2

3.1.2.  Controller vs. Device Initiated

   The framework supports both initiation models:

   *  Controller-Initiated:

      -  Controller subscribes to Energy Objects streaming.

      -  Provides centralized control over monitoring scope and
         frequency

      -  Enables dynamic adjustment of monitoring based on operational
         needs

   *  Device-Initiated:

      -  Devices can autonomously report critical energy events

      -  Useful for threshold violations or hardware failures

      -  Complements controller-initiated subscriptions

comments.author: bclaise
comments.body: This should be better in the GREEN framework, moving this open issue over there.

Should we replace "accuracy-measured-red" by "accuracy-measured-platinium" to mantain the logic of the next level in tiered systems (e.g., loyalty programs, credit cards, medals)?
        └── accuracy-measured
             ├── accuracy-measured-bronze
             ├── accuracy-measured-silver
             ├── accuracy-measured-gold
             ├── accuracy-measured-red <<<<<<<<< to be replaced by accuracy-measured-platinium?

issues.title: Accuracy and Precision: Dec 11 sync up call with the design team
issues.author: marisolpalmero
issues.body:
**Action**: Marisol to review documentation from vendors, on product specification on accuracy/precision, power factor implementation.

The idea is to include as a question for the working group on next Monday call:

Vendors SHOULD document the sensor type, calibration
method, and the expected measurement precision, as these factors are
distinct from accuracy but influence interpretation of the reported data?

**Action**: based on this, we need to consider to include clarification/terminology in the draft(s)

some relevant text to consider:

- Accuracy

Describes how close a measured or estimated power value is to the true physical value.

Examples:

“±10% of the actual power consumption”

“Derived from model, low fidelity”

“Direct sensor measurement, high accuracy”

- Precision

Describes the granularity or repeatability of the measurement — e.g., number of digits, stability of repeated reads.

Examples:

A sensor that reads 100.1 W, 100.2 W, 100.1 W is precise.

But if the real consumption is 120 W, the readings are precise but inaccurate.


Some more reasoning:

- A dataset may have high precision, low accuracy (stable but wrong).
- Or high accuracy, low precision (on average right, but noisy).
- Operators need accuracy for carbon reporting and power budgets.
- Engineers need precision to troubleshoot variations or detect anomalies.

Could it be something also to consider under Terminology or specific section (suggested text, to be discussed!!)
*  Accuracy versus Precision

   In the context of Power and Energy Monitoring, this document uses the
   term "accuracy" to describe how close a reported measurement is to the
   true physical value. Accuracy may be based on direct sensor readings,
   learned models, historic data, or manufacturer specifications.
   The "data-source-accuracy" identity hierarchy captures the expected
   error bounds or fidelity category of the reported value.

   While accuracy describes closeness to the true value, it is distinct
   from "precision", which refers to the repeatability, granularity, or
   number of valid digits in a measurement. A measurement may be highly
   precise yet inaccurate (e.g., stable readings that are offset from the
   real value), or accurate on average but with low precision (e.g.,
   noisy measurements).

   The accuracy identities defined in this module characterize the source
   and expected error bounds of the reported measurement but do not, by
   themselves, define the precision of the underlying sensor or data
   representation. Systems implementing this module MAY expose additional
   metadata such as sampling resolution, number of significant digits, or
   sensor calibration information to describe precision where relevant.

Based on first action, consider to add a question to the WG:
The "accuracy-measured" category indicates that the value originates from
a physical sensor. Vendors SHOULD document the sensor type, calibration
method, and the expected measurement precision, as these factors are
distinct from accuracy but influence interpretation of the reported data.

comments.author: marisolpalmero
comments.body:
here  some references to argument the discussion with the WG:
I list here the conclusions and reference:
* Most Telecom Standards Don’t Publish Accuracy
* Telecom standards (ITU, ATIS) focus on methodology and comparability across test labs.
* They assume proper calibration and good measurement practice — but do not define a fixed instrument accuracy requirement.
* Only ETSI explicitly codifies this in the measurement procedure text.
* The standards (except ETSI) do not themselves publish numeric accuracy requirements for vendor figures.



1. ETSI ES 203 136 — Lab Measurement Accuracy ONLY (NOT operational sensors)
https://www.etsi.org/deliver/etsi_es/203100_203199/203136/01.02.01_60/es_203136v010201p.pdf

 Does NOT apply to operational embedded sensors.

 Does NOT specify accuracy for device-internal energy telemetry.

________________________________

2. IETF BMWG Power Benchmarking — Benchmarking ONLY, no sensor accuracy
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-bmwg-powerbench/

 Does NOT define accuracy or uncertainty of operational measurements.

________________________________

3. ITU-T L.1310 — Metrics and methodology, no instrument accuracy
https://www.itu.int/rec/T-REC-L.1310

Defines energy efficiency metrics & methods.

 No explicit numerical accuracy requirements.

________________________________

4. ATIS-0600015.03 — Measurement/reporting methods, no accuracy
https://shop.standards.ie/en-ie/standards/atis-0600015-03-2023-165739_saig_atis_atis_3330614/

Defines TEER metric and reporting.

 No instrument accuracy requirements.

________________________________

5. General Metrology Reference (Useful): ISO/IEC 17025
Defines calibration, uncertainty, traceability.
 Not specific to telecom equipment.

________________________________

6. Vendor Product Documentation — NONE publish measurement accuracy for embedded sensors

Cisco ISR 1000
https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/products/collateral/routers/1000-series-integrated-services-routers-isr/datasheet-c78-739512.html
 Typical/max power only. NO accuracy.

Juniper MX301
https://www.juniper.net/content/dam/www/assets/datasheets/us/en/routers/mx301-universal-routing-platform-datasheet.pdf
 No accuracy.

Juniper PTX Power Planning
https://www.juniper.net/documentation/us/en/hardware/ptx10004/topics/topic-map/ptx10004-power-planning.html
 Calculations only. No sensor accuracy.

Arista 7050
https://www.arista.com/assets/data/pdf/Datasheets/7050S_Datasheet.pdf
 “Typical power per port”. No uncertainty.

Nokia 7750 SR
https://www.nokia.com/asset/i/207452/
 Power values are simulated (HPE Power Advisor). No accuracy.

Huawei AR Series
https://e.huawei.com/en/documents/others/4b9905a27fbe49e09d1fa68cbb5d3780
 Typical/max power. No accuracy.

Only power metering products publish accuracy:
Huawei Smart Power Sensor (has accuracy class)
https://solar.huawei.com/admin/asset/v1/pro/view/10b44cb3ac91489981d76e0f752b579c.pdf

Cisco Meraki MT40 Smart Power Controller (accuracy provided)
https://documentation.meraki.com/IoT/MT_-_Sensors/Product_Information/MT_Overviews_and_Datasheets/MT40_Datasheet_-_Smart_Power_Controller

 These are external sensors, NOT embedded NIC/ASIC/PSU measuring systems.

________________________________

FINAL CONCLUSION

→ No existing telecom standard defines accuracy/uncertainty for operational embedded power sensors.

→ No major vendor publishes accuracy/uncertainty for device-internal power telemetry.

→ ETSI ES 203 136 is the only standard specifying numeric accuracy—but ONLY for external lab instruments.

→ ITU-T, ATIS, IETF do NOT specify accuracy for operational measurements.

________________________________

issues.title: Per's feedback during the Call for Adoption
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
Hi,

I suggest that the reference RFC 4122 is updated to 9562 and
that the type yang:uuid is used instead of string for
/energy-objects/energy-entry/relationship/peer/id

The following errors are reported by pyang:

# ietf-power-and-energy.yang

ietf-power-and-energy.yang:45: warning: RFC 8407: 3.1: The IETF Trust
Copyright statement seems to be missing or is not correct (see pyang
--ietf-help for details).
ietf-power-and-energy.yang:768: warning: the module seems to use RFC
2119 keywords, but the required text from RFC 8174 is not found or is
not correct (see pyang --ietf-help for details).

Furthermore I suggest to add authors to the YANG module contact
statement.


# iana-ietf-power-and-energy.yang

iana-ietf-power-and-energy.yang:38: warning: RFC 8407: 3.1: The IETF
Trust Copyright statement seems to be missing or is not correct (see
pyang --ietf-help for details).

The name contains both iana and ietf, I suggest to drop "ietf-" from
the name.

The placeholder is "XXX" and not "XXXX", and there is no instruction
to the RFC Editor what to do with the placeholder.


Nit: The section number is not rendered properly for the
Operational Considerations section.


--

issues.title: Tony's feedback during call for adoption
issues.author: tony1athome
issues.body:
1. The notion of accuracy bands and tolerance classes seems unnecessary. We have the capability of transmitting numbers.  If I know that my measurement is +/- 23.7%, why not just say so?
2. Re: Power state.  You’ve provided a URL to the IANA page for power states.  All well and good, except that there are three different sets of power states on that page.  I think we’re using IEEE1621, so the URL should point to that. https://www.iana.org/assignments/power-state-sets/power-state-sets.xhtml#ieee1621
3. I don’t see how instantaneous power can be mandatory. Most devices today do not have power meters built-in and will not, due to cost reasons. It would make much more sense to make nameplate-power mandatory instead.

issues.title: unit-multiplier inconsistency between power and energy containers
issues.author: marisolpalmero
issues.body:
The unit-multiplier leaf is currently defined differently in the power and energy containers of ietf-power-and-energy.yang:

In container power: defined as mandatory true. Devices must always specify the scale 
In container energy: neither mandatory nor has a default value.

This asymmetry introduces an ambiguity problem. If a device reports total-energy-consumed: 1500 without including unit-multiplier, the client cannot determine whether that value represents 1500 Wh, 1500 kWh, or 1500 mWh — a potential error of factor 1000x in energy calculations, which is unacceptable for billing, reporting, and sustainability use cases.
Additionally, this inconsistency creates unnecessary client implementation complexity: the client must handle two different cases depending on whether it is reading power or energy data.
Note that unit-multiplier is intentionally not inherited from parent energy objects in the hardware containment tree. Each energy object declares its own unit-multiplier independently, so the client does not need to traverse the hardware tree to resolve the scale. This is a deliberate design choice to avoid client implementation errors of the kind described above.


**Proposed fix:**
Add a default "multiplier-units" statement to the unit-multiplier leaf in container energy:

leaf unit-multiplier {
  type identityref { base unit-multiplier; }
  **default "multiplier-units";**
  description
    "This multiplier applies to both total-energy-consumed
     and total-energy-delivered values. It determines the scale
     of the energy measurements, allowing representation of
     energy values from milliwatt-hours to gigawatt-hours
     using integer arithmetic. When not explicitly specified,
     the default value of multiplier-units (10^0 = 1) applies,
     meaning values are expressed in Watt-hours.";
}


The approach:

ietf-power-and-energy.yang: add default "multiplier-units" to unit-multiplier in container energy.
draft-ietf-green-power-and-energy-yang.md: document the difference between the mandatory unit-multiplier in power and the optional-with-default unit-multiplier in energy in the Operational Considerations section.
draft-ietf-green-framework.md: update Section 3.1.8 to reflect this design, removing the open WG discussion note.

comments.author: bclaise

issues.title: relationship peer id as union yang:uuid and string?
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
Per's discussion the GREEN mailing list regarding the relationship peer id
  module: ietf-power-and-energy
     +--ro energy-objects
     |  +--ro energy-entry* [object-id]
     |     +--ro object-id              string
     |     +--ro source-component-id?   -> /hw:hardware/component/name
     |     +--ro power
     |     |  +--ro instantaneous-power     int32
     |     |  +--ro nameplate-power?        uint32
     |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier         identityref
     |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?   identityref
     |     |  +--ro power-factor?           power-factor
     |     |  +--ro measurement-local?      boolean
     |     +--ro energy
     |     |  +--ro total-energy-consumed?    uint64
     |     |  +--ro total-energy-delivered?   uint64
     |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier?          identityref
     |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?     identityref
     |     |  +--ro measurement-local?        boolean
     |     |  +--ro certifications*           identityref
     |     +--ro power-state
     |     |  +--ro power-state-oper?   identityref
     |     +--ro relationship* [type]
     |        +--ro type    identityref
     |        +--ro peer* [id]                                  
     |           +--ro id         string                     <=================
     |           +--ro details?   string
     +--rw energy-control
        +--rw energy-entry* [object-id]
           +--rw object-id      string
           +--rw power-state
              +--rw power-state-oper?    -> /energy-objects/energy-entry/power-state/power-state-oper
              +--rw power-state-admin?   identityref

Per: I suggest that the reference RFC 4122 is updated to 9562 and
that the type yang:uuid is used instead of string for
/energy-objects/energy-entry/relationship/peer/id

Benoit: Actually, while it sounds interesting, we can not use the UUID in such as a case.
Indeed, a router (or a specific PoE interface) has not way to know the remove UUID. It's not even transmitted via something such as LLDP (or CDP).  On top of that, the remote device might not even support the ietf-hardware (RFC 8348).

The controller might know both UUIDs at each end of a relationship. However, we concluded that the relationships in the YANG module on router are read-only, basically present to only document what the router knows ... as opposed to be configured by the controller.

Per: This is fine, perhaps use type union yang:uuid and string?
I see that you already qualified when it can be a UUID and
when it MAY be some other identifier, so the text is fine.

issues.title: Accuracy discussion
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
From Tony Li

    1) I would like to propose that we stop using accuracy banding and instead just include numerical values.  Old school resistors used a color code to represent accuracy because they had only a single strip of color to work with.  Fortunately, YANG is much more expressive.  There is no need for us to hobble ourselves with bands when we are perfectly capable of expressing explicit error bounds, such at 650W ±30W or 700W ±10%.  This would be the scientifically accurate thing to do.


This issue was also covered at https://github.com/ietf-wg-green/green-design-work/issues/43

    The notion of accuracy bands and tolerance classes seems unnecessary. We have the capability of transmitting numbers. If I know that my measurement is +/- 23.7%, why not just say so?

However, this issue was closed automatically due to a link PR

issues.title: instantaneous power as mandatory or optional?
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
From Tony Li

2) Right now, we have the instantaneous power as mandatory.  How are people supposed to comply with that?  Put an active power meter on each and every component?  Many manufacturers will refuse for cost and space reasons.  I can see nameplate power as mandatory, but not instantaneous power.

issues.title: The unit-multiplier is for BOTH total-energy-consumed and total-energy-delivered: right or wrong?
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
module: ietf-power-and-energy
         +--ro energy-objects
         |  +--ro energy-entry* [object-id]
         |     +--ro object-id              string
         |     +--ro source-component-id?   -> /hw:hardware/component/name
         |     +--ro power
         |     |  +--ro instantaneous-power     int32
         |     |  +--ro nameplate-power?        uint32
         |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier         identityref
         |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?   identityref
         |     |  +--ro power-factor?           power-factor
         |     |  +--ro measurement-local?      boolean
         |     +--ro energy
         |     |  +--ro total-energy-consumed?    uint64
         |     |  +--ro total-energy-delivered?   uint64
         |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier?          identityref
         |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?     identityref
         |     |  +--ro measurement-local?        boolean
         |     |  +--ro certifications*           identityref

Thinking about which default values to apply, I stumbled upon this potentially new issue

           leaf total-energy-consumed {
             type uint64;
             units "Watt-hours";
             description
               "The total cumulative energy consumed by the energy object
               since the last reset. This value is specified as
               watt-hours with the magnitude of watt-hours (milliwatt-hours,
               kilowatt-hours, etc.) indicated separately as unit-multiplier
               in this container. This value is useful for tracking
               overall energy usage over time for billing, reporting,
               or optimization purposes.";
           }

           leaf total-energy-delivered {
             type uint64;
             units "Watt-hours";
             description
               "The total cumulative energy delivered by the energy object
               since the last reset. This value is specified as
               watt-hours with the magnitude of watt-hours (milliwatt-hours,
               kilowatt-hours, etc.) indicated separately as unit-multiplier
               in this container. This value is relevant for energy objects
               capable of generating power, such as those with renewable
               energy sources or battery backup systems, or capable of providing
               energy to other energy objects (e.g., PoE switches).";
           }
           leaf unit-multiplier {
             type identityref {
               base unit-multiplier;
             }
             description
               "This multiplier applies to both total-energy-consumed
                and total-energy-delivered values. It determines the scale
                of the energy measurements, allowing representation of
                energy values from milliwatt-hours to gigawatt-hours
                using integer arithmetic.";
           }

The unit-multiplier is for BOTH total-energy-consumed and total-energy-delivered.
What if the scale between the two is completely different?

Regards, Benoit

issues.title: YANG: object-id to energy-object-id
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
The terminology covers Energy Object but the YANG uses object-id. I believe we should be using the energy-object-id instead, in both instances belwo

  module: ietf-power-and-energy
     +--ro energy-objects
     |  +--ro energy-entry* [object-id]
     |     +--ro object-id              string
     |     +--ro source-component-id?   -> /hw:hardware/component/name
     |     +--ro power
     |     |  +--ro instantaneous-power     int32
     |     |  +--ro nameplate-power?        uint32
     |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier         identityref
     |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?   identityref
     |     |  +--ro power-factor?           power-factor
     |     |  +--ro measurement-local?      boolean
     |     +--ro energy
     |     |  +--ro total-energy-consumed?    uint64
     |     |  +--ro total-energy-delivered?   uint64
     |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier?          identityref
     |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?     identityref
     |     |  +--ro measurement-local?        boolean
     |     |  +--ro certifications*           identityref
     |     +--ro power-state
     |     |  +--ro power-state-oper?   identityref
     |     +--ro relationship* [type]
     |        +--ro type    identityref
     |        +--ro peer* [id]
     |           +--ro id         string
     |           +--ro details?   string
     +--rw energy-control
        +--rw energy-entry* [object-id]
           +--rw object-id      string
           +--rw power-state
              +--rw power-state-oper?    -> /energy-objects/energy-entry/power-state/power-state-oper
              +--rw power-state-admin?   identityref

issues.title: Link the object-id in the control tree and operational tree
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
we see the two object-id, one read and one read-write?

  module: ietf-power-and-energy
     +--ro energy-objects
     |  +--ro energy-entry* [object-id]
     |     +--ro object-id              string
     |     +--ro source-component-id?   -> /hw:hardware/component/name
     |     +--ro power
     |     |  +--ro instantaneous-power     int32
     |     |  +--ro nameplate-power?        uint32
     |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier         identityref
     |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?   identityref
     |     |  +--ro power-factor?           power-factor
     |     |  +--ro measurement-local?      boolean
     |     +--ro energy
     |     |  +--ro total-energy-consumed?    uint64
     |     |  +--ro total-energy-delivered?   uint64
     |     |  +--ro unit-multiplier?          identityref
     |     |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?
     |     |  +--ro measurement-local?        boolean
     |     |  +--ro certifications*           identityref
     |     +--ro power-state
     |     |  +--ro power-state-oper?   identityref
     |     +--ro relationship* [type]
     |        +--ro type    identityref
     |        +--ro peer* [id]
     |           +--ro id         string
     |           +--ro details?   string
     +--rw energy-control
        +--rw energy-entry* [object-id]
           +--rw object-id      string
           +--rw power-state
              +--rw power-state-oper?    -> /energy-objects/energy-entry/power-state/power-state-oper
              +--rw power-state-admin?   identityref


The two object-id values are meant to match: the read-write one under energy-control is how an admin/controller creates a control entry for a specific Energy Object (to then set power-state-admin), while the read-only one under energy-objects is the device's own report of which Energy Objects actually exist. They correlate by sharing the same string value — the config side doesn't discover object IDs, it just needs to reference the ID of an object the device is already reporting operationally.

It seems that we must be more precise in the draft to cover this aspect. 

OLD:
Finally, note that the instance is in the configuration tree, having a
required-instance false leafref to operational tree instance.

NEW:
Entries in /energy-control/energy-entry are expected to use the same
object-id value as the corresponding entry in
/energy-objects/energy-entry, allowing an administrator or controller
to correlate a configured power state intent with the Energy Object
being monitored. The configuration tree does not itself discover
object-id values; it is populated by referencing an object-id that
the device is expected to also report operationally.
Because the power-state-oper leafref under /energy-control uses
require-instance false, a configuration entry MAY be created for an
object-id for which no corresponding operational entry currently
exists (e.g., if the entry is provisioned before the device starts
reporting it, or if the Energy Object is temporarily or permanently
absent). In that case, power-state-oper simply does not resolve to
any value until a matching operational entry appears.

issues.title: The Energy Object should be in the terminology draft, not the framework
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-green-power-and-energy-yang/ version 3 mentions:

   This document makes use of the terms defined in
   [I-D.ietf-green-framework-01]

   - Energy Object

It would be easier if this term, like the other ones, would be in the terminology section.

issues.title: IANA issues from the early review
issues.author: bclaise
issues.body:
Dear Authors (cc: green),

Before the IETF meeting, we check working group agendas for documents with IANA-related issues. We have notes about this document:

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-green-power-and-energy-yang-03

It looks like this IANA Considerations section is still in progress, but I'll go ahead and point out that it currently has these issues: 

1) it doesn’t include the registration templates from RFC 9907, Section 3.8.3

2) it states that registrations should be added to the registry instead of being added directly to the module, but a table of registrations hasn’t been included.

3) The conflation of the instruction to create a registry group with the YANG module name registration in Table 1 is a little confusing, as it makes it look like we’re creating a registry that consists of the information that we also need to populate the registration of the YANG module name. The URI and YANG module name registrations should have their own subsection(s), separate from the creation of the registry group.

If you have any questions, just let us know. If you'd like to talk in person, you can find us at the IANA table from Monday through Thursday. You can also request another review at any time by contacting us at [iana@iana.org](mailto:iana@iana.org).

For more information about IANA Considerations section requirements, please see

https://www.iana.org/help/protocol-registration

Best regards,

Amanda Baber
IANA

pulls.title: Adding "mandatory true" for current-power and its unit-multiplier
pulls.author: bclaise
pulls.body:
OLD:
module: ietf-power-and-energy
  +--ro energy-objects
     +--ro energy-entry* [object-id]
        +--ro object-id              string
        +--ro source-component-id?   -> /hw:hardware/component/name
        +--ro power
        |  +--ro current-power?          int32
        |  +--ro nameplate-power?        uint32
        |  +--ro unit-multiplier?        identityref
        |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?   identityref
        |  +--ro power-factor?           power-factor
        |  +--ro measurement-local?      boolean
        +--ro energy
           +--ro total-energy-consumed?   uint64
           +--ro total-energy-produced?   uint64
           +--ro unit-multiplier?         identityref
           +--ro data-source-accuracy?    identityref
           +--ro measurement-local?       boolean


NEW:
module: ietf-power-and-energy
  +--ro energy-objects
     +--ro energy-entry* [object-id]
        +--ro object-id              string
        +--ro source-component-id?   -> /hw:hardware/component/name
        +--ro power
        |  +--ro current-power          int32
        |  +--ro nameplate-power?        uint32
        |  +--ro unit-multiplier        identityref
        |  +--ro data-source-accuracy?   identityref
        |  +--ro power-factor?           power-factor
        |  +--ro measurement-local?      boolean
        +--ro energy
           +--ro total-energy-consumed?   uint64
           +--ro total-energy-produced?   uint64
           +--ro unit-multiplier?         identityref
           +--ro data-source-accuracy?    identityref
           +--ro measurement-local?       boolean

pulls.title: including IANA considerations
pulls.author: marisolpalmero

comments.author: bclaise
comments.body:
Here is a good example of what we should be doing
See https://www.iana.org/assignments/yang-parameters/yang-parameters.xhtml => iana-hardware is "Maintained by IANA?" Yes
This YANG module only contains identities. See https://www.iana.org/assignments/iana-hardware/iana-hardware.xhtml

From https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc8348/, you can see:
  The "iana-hardware" module defines YANG identities for the hardware
   types in the IANA-maintained "IANA-ENTITY-MIB" registry.

And obviously, the ietf-hardware  YANG module import iana-hardware.

We should follow the same example in our draft.

Please clarify whether the IANA registry is a Identity Naming Registry or a Numeric Code Point Registry. It seems to be Identity Naming Registry IMO. Thanks.

pulls.title: Power state and Reference further optimizations.
pulls.author: williamchern
pulls.body:
According to authors' discussion yesterday, the following optimizations are made following Benoit's meeting minutes:
1. Remove references to EMAN (Marisol will put the clarification in Framework draft in the same time)
2. Change the reference to power-state to IANA
3. describe in the YANG model that the oper tree will be way bigger in terms of instances than the config tree (which represent the intention)
4. have a required-instance false leafref from config tree instance to oper tree instance
5. comment in the draft: NMDA design with a single "state" leaf is also a possibiliy.

According to authors' discussion yesterday, the following optimizations are made following Benoit's meeting minutes:
1.      Remove references to EMAN (Marisol will put the clarification in Framework draft in the same time)
2.      Change the reference to power-state to IANA
3.      describe in the YANG model that the oper tree will be way bigger in terms of instances than the config tree (which represent the intent)
4.      have a required-instance false leafref from config tree instance to oper tree instance (removed because of pyang lint error)
5.      comment in the draft: NMDA design with a single "state" leaf is also a possibility.

pulls.title: YANG module design choice
pulls.author: bclaise
pulls.body: Clarified the differentiation between power-state-admin and power-state-oper YANG leaves, and explained the structure of the operational and configuration trees.

comments.author: williamchern
comments.body: Looks good, I will merge this and fix the leafref issue and publish a new version based on these changes. Thanks.

```suggestion
two leaves together form the complete power state management interface.
```

```suggestion
The operational tree ('container energy-objects') will typically contain
```

```suggestion
a significantly larger number of instances than the configuration tree
```

```suggestion
('container energy-control'). The configuration tree, which is limited to
```

```suggestion
explicitly provisioned entries, provides a compact self-contained view
of the intent. For this reason, although an NMDA (Network Management
Datastore Architecture) design with a single "state" leaf (per [RFC8342])
was considered, it is not adopted in this document.

Finally, note that the instance is in the configuration tree, have a
required-instance false leafref to operational tree instance.


```

pulls.body:
Issue 37
From the May 25th 2026 meeting minutes

    Looking at Q2: Relationship between devices. Should they be configurable?
        Rob: My suggestion is no, keep relationship between devices to the controller level rather than the device level. In case of relationship between LC and switchcard this should be populated by the device directly in the operational data.
        Benoit: I'm happy with this direction.
        General agreement between others on the call.

We should have some text around

Regards, Benoit

Amanda Baber via RT <iana-issues@iana.org>
[IANA #1456118] Early review: draft-ietf-green-power-and-energy-yang-03 (IETF 126)

Hi,
Thanks to Gen for stopping by the table. The revised IANA text looks good to me.
At the table, I was wondering whether the IANA Considerations section had guidance for the designated experts, as described in RFC 8126, Section 4.5 (third paragraph). It looks like this has already been addressed by the first set of bullet points in Section 9.1.
Thanks,
Amanda
